title: Description and error analysis of quantum alghorithms in the projection evolution model -- the Deutsch algorithm case
abstract: This work demonstrates that the Deutsch algorithm can be effectively modelled using a two-level harmonic oscillator within the second quantization formalism. By adopting this framework, evolution operators are derived. We present a projection evolution model that accurately characterizes the physical state transformation within quantum gates. This approach provides a systematic method for finding evolution operators, enabling the complete description and prediction of state evolution - including projection errors - in quantum algorithms.
summary: This paper applies a novel 'projection evolution' model to the Deutsch algorithm, treating time as a quantum observable. It provides a framework for calculating how gate errors propagate, finding that the algorithm can exhibit a self-correcting behavior that reduces overall error.
main_problem: Developing a physical model to describe quantum gate dynamics and predict error propagation in the Deutsch algorithm using a projection evolution framework.
main_result: The study demonstrates that the total error of the Deutsch algorithm can be smaller than the error of a single Hadamard gate, revealing a self-correcting effect under certain error conditions.
method: The authors use second quantization formalism (creation and annihilation operators) and density matrix evolution within a Projection Evolution (PEv) model where time is treated as a quantum observable.
model_or_system: The system is a two-qubit quantum register modeled as a two-level harmonic oscillator within the second quantization formalism, implementing the Deutsch algorithm.
key_observables: Probability of correct and incorrect results (Prob(|0>) and Prob(|1>)), density matrices, and the evolution parameter tau.
important_parameters: The single-gate success probability alpha^2 and the discrete evolution steps tau_0 through tau_4.
main_assumptions: The wave function depends on all four spacetime coordinates, evolution operators are represented by an orthogonal resolution of unity, and the phase alpha is set to zero.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Deutsch algorithm circuit; Figure 2 illustrates the evolution timeline; Figure 3 plots success/failure probabilities against gate error; Figure 4 compares algorithm error to single-gate error; Figure 5 shows the ratio of these errors.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the PEv model and its application to the Deutsch algorithm, derives evolution operators for gates using second quantization, performs error propagation analysis for bit-flip errors, and concludes with a comparison of algorithm-level vs. gate-level error rates.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
